summary: Apple announced significant changes to its iOS developer agreement and published new App Store Review Guidelines, a plain-English document explaining the rules for app acceptance. The most consequential change is the removal of Section 3.3.1's ban on third-party developer tools and specific programming languages, effectively re-allowing tools like Adobe's Flash cross-compiler. The interpreter rules were simplified but not substantively changed, and the anti-AdMob language targeting Google's advertising network was quietly removed. The new Review Guidelines document is written in remarkably casual language that sounds like Steve Jobs, covering everything from fart apps to political satire exemptions. Gruber sees the guidelines as a welcome step toward transparency, codifying rules that were previously enforced but never written down.
key_points: Section 3.3.1's ban on third-party development tools and specific programming languages has been completely removed, re-allowing Flash cross-compilers and similar tools
The language that appeared specifically written to ban Google's AdMob advertising network has been stripped from the analytics and privacy section
Apple published its first plain-English App Store Review Guidelines, codifying previously unwritten rules used by reviewers
The guidelines are written in unusually casual language, including references to fart apps and Supreme Court obscenity standards, suggesting Steve Jobs's direct involvement
The document explicitly states it is 'living' and that new apps may trigger new rules at any time
money_quotes: If you can produce a binary that complies with the guidelines, how you produced it doesn't matter.
The language that seemed written specifically to ban AdMob — the mobile ad network purchased by Google, a purveyor of mobile operating systems — has been removed.
We have over 250,000 apps in the App Store. We don't need any more Fart apps.
If your App looks like it was cobbled together in a few days, or you're trying to get your first practice App into the store to impress your friends, please brace yourself for rejection.
If you run to the press and trash us, it never helps.
Not sure why 'professionals' qualify for an exemption here, nor what it is that qualifies someone as a 'professional'.
The existence of this document is a very welcome change, and it goes a long way to answering much of the criticism regarding prior controversial App Store rejections.
key_insight: Apple's decision to publish plain-English App Store guidelines and remove its bans on third-party developer tools represents a significant shift toward transparency, though the rules remain ultimately discretionary.
